issues.body: `_is_cas_conflict` returns true for any stderr containing `HTTP 422`, although 422 is not uniquely a create-if-absent race; it also represents ordinary request validation failures. This contradicts `_write_ledger`'s stated contract that validation errors raise immediately. Such a failure is retried six times and then becomes `-1`, `None`, or `False` depending on the caller, obscuring a persistent configuration/payload error as CAS contention. The self-test cements the over-broad classification by supplying only the status code and labeling it a create race.
issues.fix_hint: Distinguish the specific create-race response from other 422 responses using structured response details or a narrowly verified error signature; otherwise raise `LeaseIOError`. Add a self-test for a non-race 422 validation response that must fail loud, alongside the create-race case.
issues.title: All HTTP 422 validation failures are misclassified as CAS conflicts

issues.body: The assertion only requires `_backoff_delay` to remain within `[0, ceiling]`. Implementations that always return 0, always return the ceiling, or always return half the ceiling all pass, so the test does not establish the claimed full-jitter behavior.
issues.fix_hint: Stub `random.uniform` with a sentinel-returning fake and assert that `_backoff_delay(attempt)` calls it with exactly `(0, _backoff_ceiling(attempt))` and propagates its result.
issues.title: The full-jitter test can pass for deterministic non-jitter delays

summary: The retry/backoff placement is sound and the conflict/retry tests exercise the main control flow, but the new error classifier does not meet its fail-loud contract: it treats every HTTP 422 response as a CAS race. GitHub can return 422 for non-race validation failures, so those failures are silently converted into retries and ultimately into the callers' generic CAS-exhaustion result. The jitter self-test also checks only bounds, not that full jitter is actually used.
